explanation: The requirement states that whenever prop_0 holds, prop_1 must also hold. In LTL, this is typically captured globally using G (globally): at all times, if prop_0 is true, then prop_1 must also be true.
The LTL formula captures that whenever prop_0 is true, prop_1 must also be true at that point in time. This is globally required since it holds at all times.
This formula enforces that whenever prop_0 holds, prop_1 must also hold at the same time step. This is captured by a global implication: globally, if prop_0 holds, then prop_1 must hold.
This formula expresses that globally, whenever prop_0 holds, prop_1 must also hold at the same moment. This is written as a global implication: G(prop_0 -> prop_1).
This formula states that globally, whenever prop_0 holds, prop_1 must also hold at the same time. This captures the implication 'If prop_0, then prop_1.' at all points in time.
The specification states that at all times, if prop_0 holds, then prop_1 must also hold. This requires a globally (G) operator with an implication from prop_0 to prop_1.
The statement expresses that whenever prop_0 holds in a state, prop_1 must also hold in that state. In LTL, this is expressed globally: for all time points, if prop_0, then prop_1.
The statement expresses that whenever 'prop_0' is true, 'prop_1' must also be true at that point. Globally, it should always hold that if 'prop_0' holds, so does 'prop_1'.

This expresses that globally (at all times), if prop_0 holds, then prop_1 must also hold. This captures the if-then relationship between the two atomic propositions according to standard LTL semantics.
This states that whenever prop_0 holds, prop_1 must also hold at the same time, for all times throughout the execution.
